coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence sensing strategy for xanthine assay based on gold nanoclusters and nanozyme
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mengjun
Zhang, Jiabao
Zhou, Xiaobin
Sun, Huilin
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: Making the gold-thiolate shell rigid is an appealing and efficient strategy for improving the fluorescence of gold nanoclusters (GNCs). In this study, ultrabright lysozyme-functionalized 5-methyl-2-thiouracil gold nanoclusters (MT-LZ@GNCs) with yellow emission were rationally designed and synthesized based on MT@GNCs by making the NC shell modified with the lysozyme. The resultant MT-LZ@GNCs exhibited a remarkable luminescence efficiency, high stability, and water-solubility. By combining MT-LZ@GNCs with iron-doped carbon-nanosheet (Fe/C NS) that mimicked peroxidase-like activity, a novel fluorescent nanoprobe was developed for the determination of xanthine. Under the catalysis of xanthine oxidase (XOD), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was produced during the oxidation of xanthine. In the presence of H2O2, Fe/C NS can effectively catalyze H2O2 to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and subsequently catalyze p-phenylenediamine (PPD) to form its oxidized product (PPDox), thereby quenching the fluorescence of MT-LZ@GNCs at 550 nm via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Coupling the peroxidase-like activity of Fe/C NS and XOD cascade reactions, the proposed sensing platform based on MT-LZ@GNCs and Fe/C NS can realize the quantitative analysis of xanthine in the range from 0.5 to 400 µmol L−1, affording a low detection limit of 0.23 µmol L−1. In addition, this study provided satisfactory results for monitoring the xanthine content of real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Gold nanoclusters
Nanozyme
Hydrogen peroxide
Xanthine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of MT-LZ@GNCs and Fe/C NS

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21775052 and 21575048 ), the Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province, China (No. 20180414013GH ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of MT-LZ@GNCs (Inset exhibits the photographs of MT-LZ@GNCs under 365 nm UV irradiation (left) and sunlight (right)). (b) Fluorescence spectra of the MT-LZ@GNCs under varied excitations. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of MT@GNCs and MT-LZ@GNCs. (d) High-resolution TEM image of MT-LZ@GNCs and corresponding particle size distribution.
(a) FT-IR spectra of MT@GNCs and MT-LZ@GNCs. (b) XPS survey spectrum of MT-LZ@GNCs. Au 4f (c) and S 2p (d) XPS spectra of MT-LZ@GNCs.

(a) Synthesis process of Fe/C NS. (b) TEM image of Fe/C NS. (c) XRD pattern of Fe/C NS. (d) UV–vis spectra of the Fe/C NS/TMB/H2O2 system containing varied concentrations of KSCN. (e) CV responses of Fe/C NS and Fe/C NS/H2O2. Inset: CV responses of bare GCE and H2O2.

(a) Schematic illustration of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD-based biosensor for xanthine assay. (b) Fluorescence spectra of MT-LZ@GNCs, MT-LZ@GNCs + PPD, MT-LZ@GNCs + Fe/C NS, and MT-LZ@GNCs + PPD + Fe/C NS + H2O2 system. (c) Fluorescence responses of Fe/C NS/PPD/MT-LZ@GNCs system toward xanthine, XOD and xanthine/XOD, respectively. (d) UV–vis spectra of PPDox; fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of MT-LZ@GNCs. (e) Stern–Volmer curves for the MT-LZ@GNCs/PPDox system at two different temperatures. (f) Time-resolved fluorescence decays of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/xanthine/XOD and MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/xanthine/XOD/PPD system.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD system with the addition of H2O2 (0, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600 μmol L−1). Inset: plotting the FL intensity versus H2O2 concentration. (b) Fluorescence spectra of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD system with the addition of xanthine (0, 0.5, 2.5, 5, 15, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 500 μmol L−1). (c) Plotting the FL intensity versus xanthine concentration. (d) The normalized fluorescence intensity of the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD system with xanthine or varied interfering substances. Conditions: 200 μmol L−1 xanthine, 2 mmol ions, and 1.2 mmol L−1 other biomolecules.
(a) Schematic representation of the fabrication of MT-LZ@GNCs. (b) Illustration of the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS-based fluorescence sensing strategy for xanthine analysis.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence sensing strategy for xanthine assay based on gold nanoclusters and nanozyme

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Making the gold-thiolate shell rigid is an appealing and efficient strategy for improving the fluorescence of gold nanoclusters (GNCs). In this study, ultrabright lysozyme-functionalized 5-methyl-2-thiouracil gold nanoclusters (MT-LZ@GNCs) with yellow emission were rationally designed and synthesized based on MT@GNCs by making the NC shell modified with the lysozyme. The resultant MT-LZ@GNCs exhibited a remarkable luminescence efficiency, high stability, and water-solubility. By combining MT-LZ@GNCs with iron-doped carbon-nanosheet (Fe/C NS) that mimicked peroxidase-like activity, a novel fluorescent nanoprobe was developed for the determination of xanthine. Under the catalysis of xanthine oxidase (XOD), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was produced during the oxidation of xanthine. In the presence of H2O2, Fe/C NS can effectively catalyze H2O2 to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and subsequently catalyze p-phenylenediamine (PPD) to form its oxidized product (PPDox), thereby quenching the fluorescence of MT-LZ@GNCs at 550 nm via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Coupling the peroxidase-like activity of Fe/C NS and XOD cascade reactions, the proposed sensing platform based on MT-LZ@GNCs and Fe/C NS can realize the quantitative analysis of xanthine in the range from 0.5 to 400 µmol L−1, affording a low detection limit of 0.23 µmol L−1. In addition, this study provided satisfactory results for monitoring the xanthine content of real samples.

Schematic representation of the fabrication of MT-LZ@GNCs (a). Illustration of the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS-based fluorescence sensing strategy for xanthine analysis (b).
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: As a type of prospective fluorescent nanomaterials, metal nanoclusters (NCs) have garnered extensive attention in catalysis, sensing, and bioimaging applications due to their distinct sub-nanometer dimensions, tunable fluorescence emission, and good biocompatibility [1,2]. Thus far, numerous fluorescent NCs have been designed and synthesized. At the same time, the size, structure, and composition of these NCs can be tuned by metal-metal and metal-ligand bonding capabilities [3,4]. Typically, the post-modification and molecular functionalization of some NCs with thiol-containing small molecules as coating agents is difficult owing to the lack of abundant functional groups, which could restrict their applications in fluorescence assays. By contrast, proteins (e.g., bovine serum albumin and papain) with abundant amino acid residues can protect NCs more efficiently and improve their optical properties [5,6]. For example, Zhu et al. have reported the fluorescence enhancement of 6-methyl-2-thiouracil-protected Au NCs by the introduction of lysozyme [7]. Mi et al. have prepared dual ligand-capped bimetallic nanoclusters (GSH-Lys@Ag-AuNCs) [8]. Therefore, it is desirable to prepare novel and intense fluorescent nanoclusters via the structure regulation of NCs.

Xanthine, a key metabolite of adenine nucleotide degradation, is extensively distributed in the human body, body fluids and biological organs. It is commonly utilized as a mild stimulant and bronchodilator, especially for the treatment of asthma symptoms. Previous reports had confirmed that the abnormal purine metabolism in human body was related to various diseases, including xanthinuria, cerebral ischemia, tumor hyperthermia and adult respiratory distress syndrome. Therefore, construction of a sensitive, selective, and reliable sensing strategy for xanthine assay is of great significance for the early diagnosis of related diseases. Under the catalysis of xanthine oxidase (XOD), the level of xanthine can be estimated by determining the generated H2O2 using electrochemical [9], colorimetric [10], fluorometric [11], and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [12] means. Among these analytical protocols, by owing the advantages of easy operation, low cost-effectiveness, and excellent sensitivity, the fluorometric method has gained increasing attention in recent years. Xu et al. have constructed a fluorescent sensing strategy for xanthine analysis based on the MIL-100(Fe)/three-dimensional graphene nanocomposites (MIL-3DG-75) with peroxidase-like catalytic activity [13]. Su et al. have designed a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-channel nanoprobe based on hemin@carbon dot hybrid nanozymes (hemin@CD) for xanthine analysis [14]. Despite progress, developing optical probe for xanthine assay with high accuracy and sensitivity is still urgently desired.

In this study, fluorescence-enhanced lysozyme-functionalized 5-methyl-2-thiouracil gold nanoclusters (MT-LZ@GNCs) were achieved by rigidifying the shell of MT@GNCs with lysozyme ( Scheme 1(a)). The fluorescence emission peak of MT-LZ@GNCs was observed at 550 nm upon excitation at 430 nm. Utilizing MT-LZ@GNCs as a fluorescent indicator and Fe-doped carbon-nanosheet (Fe/C NS) as a peroxidase substitute, a novel fluorescent sensing approach to evaluate the xanthine content was established (Scheme 1(b)). And the quenching mechanism of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS-integrated biosensor was completely investigated and confirmed by analyzing the Stern–Volmer equation, absorbance spectra, MT-LZ@GNCs-PPDox distance and fluorescence decay curves. Briefly, with the catalysis of xanthine oxidase (XOD) in the presence of oxygen (O2), xanthine was transformed into uric acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Fig. S1). After the addition of peroxidase-like activity Fe/C NS, H2O2 can be decomposed into reactive oxygen species (ROS, O2 ·−/1O2), which can further catalyze the oxidation of p-phenylenediamine (PPD) to its oxidation product (PPDox) with absorbance in the range of 370–630 nm. And the fluorescence intensity of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD was quenched by the formed PPDox through Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Overall, the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS-integrated fluorescent sensing platform for xanthine assay was realized in a sensitive and reliable manner.
Reagents and instruments have been provided in the Supplementary Material.

Primarily, 200 µL of HAuCl4 (25 mmol L−1) was added into a mixed solution of 180 µL of MT (50 mmol L−1) and 20 µL of sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 1.4 mol L−1) to form a light-yellow transparent solution after stirring at room temperature for 1 h. Subsequently, 200 µL of fresh prepared solution of lysozyme (20 mg mL−1) was added. After stirring for 2 h, yellow-emitting MT-LZ@GNCs were obtained.

The procedure for fabricating Fe/C NS was based on our previous report [15]. Firstly, the precursor zinc/iron-based metal-organic framework material (Zn/Fe MOF) was prepared. 150 mg of 1,4,5,8-naphthalenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (NTDA) was dissolved in 125 mL of NaOH (9 mmol L−1) and stirred at 40 °C for 6 h. Then, 10 mL of deionized water was utilized to dissolve 750 mg of Zn (NO3)2.6 H2O, and then 20 mg of FeSO4.7 H2O was introduced to form Zn/Fe mixed solution in a ratio of 75:2. After stirring at 60 °C for 24 h, the resulting light-yellow solid was collected, washed, and then dried. Subsequently, Fe/C NS was prepared by high-temperature pyrolysis. Briefly, Zn/Fe MOF (0.1 g) and dicyandiamide (DCDA, 1.0 g) were mixed. After the resultant mixture was sufficiently ground, the mixture was subjected to pyrolysis under a N2 flow at 800 °C for 3 h. Finally, pure Fe/C NS was obtained by leaching the pyrolyzed product using 2 mol L−1 HNO3 at 80 °C for 5 h and washing 5 times with deionized water. Additionally, C NS was synthesized in the same procedures as the Fe/C NS but without the additional of DCDA and FeSO4·7H2O.

For the detection of xanthine, 100 µL xanthine with varied concentrations, 50 µL XOD (10 U mL−1), and 50 µL phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 8, 100 mmol L−1) were added into a 2 mL centrifugal tube, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 25 min. Then, 20 µL PPD (15 mmol L−1), 25 µL Fe/C NS (50 µg mL−1) and 100 µL PBS (pH = 7.3, 100 mmol L−1) were introduced. After keeping in the dark for 20 min, 30 µL MT-LZ@GNCs and 625 µL H2O were added to the mixture and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded (λex = 430 nm).

Human blood sample, contributed by the Jilin University China-Japan Union Hospital, was selected as the real sample to estimate the practical applicability of this fluorescence nanoprobe. After centrifugation at 11,250 r/min for 18 min, the supernatant was collected and utilized as the serum samples. At the same time, various levels of xanthine (15, 100, 300 μmol L−1) were mixed with the serum to prepare spiked samples. Then, the assay of xanthine in serum samples was carried out as depicted above (Section 2.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MT-LZ@GNCs and Fe/C NS
ce:section.ce:para: As a bacteriostatic protein containing a certain amount of arginine residues, lysozyme is widely distributed in fluid samples of saliva, tears, and serum [16,17]. Owing to its superb biocompatible, lysozyme has been widely utilized as a stabilizer to prepare nanoparticles [18] and NCs, etc. [19,20]. Enlighted by the secondary structure and composition of lysozyme, yellow-emitting MT-LZ@GNCs were prepared by a facile self-assembly strategy. The CO and NH groups of MT as the capping agent were expected to form hydrogen bonds with the guanidyl groups of lysozyme decorated MT-LZ@GNCs [21]. The formed hydrogen bonds endowed MT-LZ@GNCs with improved stability and optical properties compared with those of monodispersed MT@GNCs [7]. Meanwhile, the quantum yield of MT-LZ@GNCs was determined to be 19.93%. Fig. 1a shows the MT-LZ@GNCs with two obvious absorption peaks at 273 nm and 430 nm. The absorption band at 273 nm was assigned to the π-π*transition [22]. And the absorption peak at 430 nm was assigned to the n–π* transition [23,24]. The fluorescence spectra of MT-LZ@GNCs (Fig. 1a) exhibits a fluorescence emission peak at 550 nm upon excitation at 370/430 nm. According to previous reports [25], the excitation peaks at 273 nm and 430 nm were ascribed to the π-π*transition and n-π* transition, respectively. Therefore, it is reasonable to speculate that this dual excitation-one emission phenomenon was attributed to the hyperconjugation of its two fluorescence centers. Furthermore, the MT-LZ@GNCs exhibited excitation-independent fluorescence emissions (Fig. 1b). Compared with that of MT@GNCs, the fluorescence intensity of MT-LZ@GNCs was enhanced by ~ 3.67 times (Fig. 1c). The morphologies of MT@GNCs and MT-LZ@GNCs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). MT@GNCs presented a near-spherical nanostructure with a mean size of 1.51 nm (Fig. S2). By contrast, owing to the incorporation of lysozyme, the size of monodispersed MT-LZ@GNCs increased slightly (approximately 1.71 nm). Besides, the lattice spacing of MT-LZ@GNCs was found to be 0.235 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane of metallic Au (Fig. 1d).

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy further confirmed the functional groups on MT-LZ@GNCs and MT@GNCs. Characteristic peaks of MT@GNCs in the vicinity of 1649, 1535, and 1337 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CO, N–H, and C–N, respectively ( Fig. 2a). After the treatment of MT@GNCs with lysozyme, the absorption band of the resultant MT-LZ@GNCs was found to be enhanced at 1535 cm−1 and shifted at 1649 cm−1, which could be attributed to the formation of novel bonds between MT and lysozyme, resulting in structural changes in the MT@GNCs [21]. In addition, the fluorescence of MT-LZ@GNCs was temperature-dependent and reversible (Fig. S3), further revealing the existence of hydrogen bonds [7]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) profiles presented in Fig. 2b revealed that MT-LZ@GNCs comprise Au, C, N, O and S. Au (4f) XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c) of MT-LZ@GNCs was divided into two distinct components located at 87.81 and 84.23 eV, respectively [26]. Characteristic peaks corresponded to Au 4f7/2 (84.36 eV) and Au 4f5/2 (88.10 eV) signals of Au(0) and to Au 4f5/2 (87.61 eV) and Au 4f7/2 (83.92 eV) signals of Au(I), respectively [27]. Simultaneously, the S 2p spectrum in Fig. 2d exhibited three peaks of oxidized S (167.84 eV), unbonded S (164.51 eV) and Au-S (163.23 eV), which suggested the MT offers thiol groups and forms AuS bonds with Au(I) to stabilize the MT-LZ@GNCs. The above results revealed that MT-LZ@GNCs were successfully synthesized.

Fig. 3a shows the preparation procedure of Fe/C NS. After high-temperature pyrolysis, Fe/C NS was prepared using Zn/Fe MOF and DCDA as the precursors. The TEM image in Fig. 3b revealed that Fe/C NS exhibits a nanosheet structure. The degree of graphitization of Fe/C NS was confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). The (002) and (101) peaks at 25.6° and 44.3°, respectively, were observed in the XRD pattern of Fe/C NS, and Fe characteristic peaks were absent [28], indicating that the precursors were completely carbonized into carbon-based nanomaterials during pyrolysis (Fig. 3c). As illustrated in Fig. S4, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was oxidized to blue in the solution of Fe/C NS/H2O2, reversely, no obvious absorbance changes were observed in individual H2O2, C NS, or Fe/C NS system, manifesting the favorable peroxidase-like activity of Fe/C NS as well as the importance of Fe doping. The absorbance intensity of Fe/C NS/H2O2/TMB system decrease distinctly upon the addition of KSCN, demonstrating the Fe-Nx sites acting as active sites (Fig. 3d) [29]. Our previous study has illustrated that the Fe/C NS can catalyze H2O2 to produce ROS (O2 ·− and 1O2), which can further catalyze the oxidation of substrate to its oxidation product [15]. Furthermore, we performed cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis to examine the electrochemical reduction behavior of H2O2 [30]. As presented in Fig. 3e, compared with the bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE), the current of Fe/C NS-modified GCE was apparently increased, manifesting Fe/C NS could effectively promote electron transfer. Subsequently, to estimate the catalytical performance of Fe/C NS, steady-state kinetic analysis was performed with TMB and H2O2 as substrates [31,32]. The maximum initial velocity (Vmax) (5.87 × 10−8 mol L−1 s−1 for H2O2 and 9.29 × 10−8 mol L−1 s−1 for TMB) and Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) (0.32 mmol L−1 for H2O2 and 0.092 mmol L−1 for TMB) were obtained from the Lineweaver-Burk plots (Fig. S5). The Km value of Fe/C NS was much lower and the Vmax value was higher than those of some reported nanozymes (Table S1), suggesting the favorable affinity of Fe/C NS for the substrate molecules, and the maximum reaction rate can be reached at a low substrate concentration. Another key parameter is the catalytic constant (Kcat), and the higher Kcat value reflects higher catalytic efficiency of enzyme [33]. The derived Kcat values of Fe/C NS were better than, or at least comparable to those of other reported nanozymes (Table S1), suggesting the favorable catalytical performance of Fe/C NS.

The construction process of the fluorometric sensing strategy was presented in Fig. 4a, which integrated the yellow-emitting MT-LZ@GNCs and xanthine-regulated oxidation of PPD. Meanwhile, several control experiments were conducted to confirm the feasibility of the fluorometric sensing strategy. PPD, Fe/C NS, and Fe/C NS/H2O2 system exhibited a negligible effect on the fluorescence of MT-LZ@GNCs (Figs. 4b and S6). However, with the coexistence of Fe/C NS, H2O2 and PPD, the fluorescence of MT-LZ@GNCs clearly decreased, indicating that the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD-based fluorometric probe was successfully constructed (Fig. 4b). In the presence of xanthine, XOD can catalyze the oxidation of xanthine to generate H2O2, and Fe/C NS with peroxidase-mimicking activity can catalyze H2O2 to produce ROS, which can further promote the oxidation of PPD to PPDox and result in the fluorescence quenching of MT-LZ@GNCs (Fig. 4c). The above results confirmed the feasibility of the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD-based sensing system for xanthine assay.

It can be observed that there was a considerable spectral overlap between the excitation/emission spectrum of MT-LZ@GNCs and absorbance spectrum of PPDox (Fig. 4d), which suggested possibility for FRET or inner-filter effect (IFE) between MT-LZ@GNCs and PPDox. To further verify the quenching mechanism in the sensing system, Stern–Volmer (SV) analysis was performed. The equation was expressed as follows [34]:
F0/F = 1+kqτ0[Q] = 1+Ksv[Q]; F0/F = τ0/τ
where kq is the quenching constant, [Q] is the PPDox concentration, and Ksv is the SV quenching coefficient. F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensities of MT-LZ@GNCs with or without PPDox, respectively, and τ and τ0 represent the lifetime of MT-LZ@GNCs with or without PPDox, respectively [35,36]. Basically, in static quenching, the fluorescence lifetime of the excited state is supposedly constant in terms of the quencher density (τ0/τ = 1), conversely, in dynamic quenching, the lifetime decreases versus the quencher density according to the collisional events (τ0/τ = F0/F) [37,38]. In our experiments, the signal intensity ratio F0/F exhibited a good linearity with a PPDox content from 0 to 40 μmol L−1 (Fig. 4e), and Ksv values increased with the increase in temperature (Table S2). Therefore, the IFE quenching mechanism can be ruled out. Moreover, fluorescence lifetime of MT-LZ@GNCs in the presence and absence of PPDox and the distance between MT-LZ@GNCs and PPDox were investigated to verify the FRET mechanism. The distance between MT-LZ@GNCs and PPDox was calculated to be 5.48 nm (Section 1.2, Support information), which was consistent with the requirements for the occurrence of FRET. As presented in Fig. 4f and Table S3, the fluorescence lifetime of the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/xanthine/XOD system decreased from 8.68 ns to 6.76 ns after addition of PPD, indicating the occurrence of FRET between PPDox and MT-LZ@GNCs. All these results confirmed that PPDox could quenching the fluorescence of MT-LZ@GNCs through FRET process.

Before the quantitative assay of xanthine, some key parameters were optimized to improve the analytical performance. Firstly, the reaction time between Fe/C NS/H2O2 and PPD was optimized. The fluorescence intensity of the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/H2O2/PPD system decreased, and then stabilized when the reaction time exceeded 25 min (Fig. S7). Thus, 25 min reaction time was chosen for subsequent experiments. Meanwhile, a XOD content of 0.5 U mL−1 was chosen for xanthine detection experiments (Fig. S8). Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD system decreased with the increment in the H2O2 concentration ( Fig. 5a). Meanwhile, as shown in the inset of Fig. 5a, the fluorescence response was inversely proportional to the levels of H2O2 and two satisfactory linear relationships were obtained. The corresponding regression equations were F = 5831.94–35.24[H2O2] (R2 = 0.948) in the H2O2 range of 0.5–50 µmol L−1, and F = 4424.36–6.92[H2O2] (R2 = 0.991) in the H2O2 range of 50–500 µmol L−1. The detection limit (LOD) was determined to be 0.17 µmol L−1 (S/N = 3) [39].

Based on XOD-triggered catalytic oxidation of xanthine into H2O2, a fluorescence sensing strategy for xanthine assay was established by the integration of Fe/C NS and MT-LZ@GNCs. With the increase of the content of xanthine, the fluorescence intensity of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD system decreased systematically (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence signal of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD system exhibited good linearity in the 0.5–400 µmol L−1 xanthine concentration range (Fig. 5c). The corresponding regression equations were F = 7668.62–32.97[xanthine] (R2 = 0.984) in the xanthine range of 0.5–50 µmol L−1, and F = 6260.52–7.88[xanthine] (R2 = 0.991) in the xanthine range of 50–400 µmol L−1. The plot of FL intensity versus xanthine concentration consisted of two linear segments with different slopes. And the difference in slopes may be due to kinetic constraints [40,41]. In the low concentration range of xanthine, less H2O2 can be generated, and the produced H2O2 can be quickly depleted, leading to high sensitivity of the fluorescence response. However, the adequate H2O2 could be generated at high xanthine levels, and the Fe/C NS will interact with H2O2 for a longer time (due to the relatively small number of active sites [42]), resulting in the decrease in sensitivity. The LOD was determined to be 0.23 µmol L−1. Table S4 shows the results for xanthine determination in comparison with that of the early reported sensing strategies, demonstrating that the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD-based fluorometric method exhibits a higher sensitivity for xanthine assay.

GSH exhibits favorable reducibility and powerful ROS scavenging capacity, therefore, the produced ROS in the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/xanthine/XOD system can be consumed and triggered the fluorescence recovery of the sensing system. Fig. S9a showed that the fluorescence intensity of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/xanthine/XOD increases with GSH concentration in the range of 1–30 μmol L−1 and can be fitted by the relationship F = 159.40[GSH] + 1815.86 (R2 = 0.994) (Fig. S9b). The calculated LOD for GSH was 0.32 μmol L−1.

To assess the selectivity of the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD-based sensing method for xanthine assay, the fluorescence intensity of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD system toward various interference substances, including ions, reducing agents, and biomolecules were investigated and compared with the fluorescence intensity with xanthine (200 μmol L−1). Fig. 5d exhibits the fluorescence intensity of the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD system with xanthine dramatically decreased in comparison with those with interfering substances and blank sample, indicating this proposed sensing system possessed a satisfactory selectivity for xanthine assay.

For evaluating the practicability of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD-based fluorometric system for xanthine assay in real samples, the fluorescence response of MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD system with varied concentrations of xanthine in human serum samples were investigated. The standard addition approach by spiking different levels of xanthine to serum sample was utilized to calculate the recovery. The obtained recoveries were within the scope of 98.72–109.27% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.79–2.61% (Table S5), manifesting the satisfactory practicality and reliability of the designed biosensor in the analysis of complex samples. Additionally, the results of xanthine analysis with the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD-based fluorometric sensing strategy were very close to the values by the colorimetric sensing strategy (Table S5 and Fig. S10), illustrating the favorable practicability and accuracy of the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS-based sensing strategy for xanthine assay.

In this study, yellow-emitting MT-LZ@GNCs with excellent stability and luminescence efficiency were strategically designed and synthesized. And a novel fluorescent sensing platform comprising MT-LZ@GNCs and Fe/C NS was established for xanthine determination with excellent sensitivity and anti-interfering ability. Under optimum conditions, the MT-LZ@GNCs/Fe/C NS/PPD/XOD-based fluorescent sensing system for xanthine assay showed a dynamic detection range from 0.5 µ to 400 µmol L−1, and satisfactory results for xanthine detection were obtained in human serum samples, exhibiting promise of MT-LZ@GNCs in biological applications. The proposed biosensing system based on MT-LZ@GNCs and Fe/C NS not only provides novel inspirations for xanthine-related assay, but also broadens the application prospects of nanocluster/carbon-based nanozyme in biological analytical fields.

Mengjun Wang: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Jiabao Zhang: Formal analysis. Zhou Xiaobin: Software. Huilin Sun: Data curation. Xingguang Su: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21775052 and 21575048), the Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province, China (No. 20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131488.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mengjun Wang is currently carrying out his Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the synthesis of metal nanoclusters and their applications in bioanalysis.

Jiabao Zhang is currently carrying out his Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). His research is mainly focused on the synthesis of quantum dots and their applications for biosensors.

Xiaobin Zhou is currently carrying out his Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). His research is mainly focused on the synthesis of nanozyme and their applications.

Huilin Sun is currently carrying out her Master's degree under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). Her research is mainly focused on the metal nanoclusters and their applications for biosensors.

Xingguang Su is a professor at the Department of Analytical Chemistry at the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. She received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 1992 and her Ph.D. degree from Jilin University (China) in 1999. Her research focuses on the synthesis, characterization, functionalization and application of quantum dots and quantum dots-tagged microspheres in biomedicine.
